sensors.title: What are IoT Sensors?
sensors.subtitle: IoT (Internet of Things) sensors are small electronic devices that continuously measure environmental conditions and send data to the internet in real time.

1.title: Physical Measurement
1.desc: The sensor detects a physical parameter (temperature, particle, humidity) using a specific transducer (e.g.: thermistor, photodetector).

2.title: Analog→Digital Conversion
2.desc: The analog signal is converted to digital by an ADC (Analog-Digital Converter). Resolution defines measurement precision (e.g.: 12-bit = 4096 values).

3.title: Microcontroller Processing
3.desc: An embedded microcontroller (ESP8266, Arduino) reads the ADC value, applies calibration formulas and sends via Wi-Fi or serial.

4.title: Transmission via Internet
4.desc: Data is sent to collection APIs (Sensor.Community, Open-Meteo) via HTTP/MQTT. Interval: 1 to 5 minutes.

5.title: Collection and Storage
5.desc: EcoSense backend fetches data every hour, stores in MongoDB Atlas and calculates the ICAU-D index for each sensor.

sensors.network1Desc: Citizens' network with thousands of low-cost outdoor sensors distributed globally. Focus on air quality and urban environment.
sensors.network2Desc: Global weather forecast API aggregating data from official meteorological stations. Covers 48 fixed cities (18 BR + 30 EU).
maintenanceItems: Periodic sensor cleaning (dust accumulates on optical chamber)
Stable power supply (voltage fluctuations affect readings)
limitationItems: Low-cost sensors: ±15-20% accuracy vs. reference stations
Optical interference in fog or high humidity (>90%)
Cross-sensitivity: some gases can interfere with PM measurement
validationItems: EcoSense filters sensors inactive for >2 hours
Readings outside physical limits are discarded (temp <-50°C or >60°C)
City aggregation uses arithmetic mean, reducing isolated outlier impact
sensors.ctaText: Join the Sensor.Community and install a sensor at your home. All data is public and helps improve air quality monitoring globally.

explainer.title: How the Platform Works
explainer.subtitle: EcoSense is a real-time environmental monitoring platform. It collects data from globally distributed sensors, calculates the ICAU-D index, and displays it on maps, charts, and rankings.

architecture.title: Platform Architecture
architecture.overviewText1: EcoSense is a containerized full-stack application composed of a Node.js backend and a React frontend, communicating via REST API. All data persistence is done in MongoDB Atlas (cloud).

1.title: Collection from External APIs
1.desc: At startup and every hour, the backend fetches data from 2 public sources: Sensor.Community (air quality — ~200 global sensors) and Open-Meteo (meteorology — 48 cities in Brazil and Europe). Automatic retry with backoff on network failure.

2.title: Normalization to Unified Schema
2.desc: Heterogeneous data from sources is converted to a standard sensor object: id, source, location {lat, lon, city, country}, measurements {temperature, humidity, pm25, pm10, windSpeed}, lastSeen. Sensors with data older than 2 hours are discarded.

3.title: ICAU-D Index Calculation
3.desc: For each active sensor, ICAU-D is calculated with dynamic weights: temperature (40%), humidity (30%), air quality/PM2.5 (20%), wind (10%). If a component has no data, its weight is redistributed proportionally among available components.

4.title: Persistence in MongoDB Atlas
4.desc: All readings are saved to the SensorReading collection with a 30-day TTL (automatic expiry index). This forms the history that feeds the '7 days' and '30 days' filters in the Dashboard and Ranking.

5.title: Reverse Geocoding via Nominatim
5.desc: Sensor.Community sensor coordinates are converted to city names using the Nominatim API (OpenStreetMap). 1 req/s rate limit is respected with a serialized queue. Results are stored in MongoDB (GeocodeEntry collection) — never re-queried for the same coordinate.

6.title: City Aggregation and Cache
6.desc: Sensors with resolved cities are grouped. For each city: measurement averages, aggregated ICAU-D, ranking by score. Results are cached in MongoDB (CacheEntry collection, 1-hour TTL) for instant frontend response.

7.title: Delivery to Frontend via REST API
7.desc: React frontend queries /api/sensors, /api/cities, and /api/cities/ranking. In production, requests go through Nginx (reverse proxy). React Query manages client-side cache (55 min stale time). If live data hasn't arrived yet, the frontend automatically uses recent MongoDB history.

frontendStack.title: 🖥️ Frontend
items: React 18 + Vite — SPA with optimized build
React Query — data cache, retry, and sync

infraStack.title: 📦 Data & Infrastructure
items: TTL Index — auto-delete readings older than 30 days
Nginx — serves React and proxies /api to backend

securityStack.title: 🔒 Security & Resilience
items: Trust Proxy — correct X-Forwarded-For behind Nginx
Retry with backoff — external APIs with 3 attempts
Cache fallback — local memory if MongoDB unavailable
Public data — no personal user data collected

icaud.title: ICAU-D — Dynamic Urban Environmental Comfort Index
icaud.whatText1: ICAU-D stands for Dynamic Urban Environmental Comfort Index. It is an index that measures environmental comfort in urban areas by combining multiple environmental variables into a single value between 0 and 100.
icaud.whatText2: The higher the index, the better the comfort. A score of 80+ means the environment is very comfortable for outdoor activities.
icaud.exampleSubtitle: With: Temperature 25°C, Humidity 60%, PM2.5 15 µg/m³, Wind 3 m/s
icaud.dynamicText: When sensors don't have all 4 components (common in Open-Meteo, which has no PM2.5), the ICAU-D automatically redistributes weights proportionally among available components. This prevents sensors with partial data from always getting low scores.
icaud.dynamicExample: Example: Without PM2.5 data (weight 0.2), the remaining components are: temperature (0.4/0.8=50%), humidity (0.3/0.8=37.5%), wind (0.1/0.8=12.5%).

temperature.name: Temperature
temperature.formula:
if T between 18-26: score = 100
if T < 18: score = 100 - (18 - T) × 4
if T > 26: score = 100 - (T - 26) × 4
Range: [0, 100]

humidity.name: Humidity
humidity.formula:
if H between 40-60: score = 100
if H < 40: score = 100 - (40 - H) × 2
if H > 60: score = 100 - (H - 60) × 2
Range: [0, 100]

airQuality.name: Air Quality (PM2.5)
airQuality.formula:
score = max(0, 100 - PM2.5 × 2)
Range: [0, 100]
0 µg/m³ → 100 | 50 µg/m³ → 0

wind.name: Wind Speed
wind.formula:
if V between 1-3: score = 100
if V < 1: score = 100 - (1 - V) × 20
if V > 3: score = 100 - (V - 3) × 20
Range: [0, 100]

sources.title: Data Sources
sources.subtitle: EcoSense uses 2 free public APIs, requiring no API key.

sc.name: Sensor.Community
sc.desc: Decentralized network of thousands of low-cost sensors installed by citizens globally. Focus on PM2.5, PM10, temperature and humidity.

meteo.name: Open-Meteo
meteo.desc: Open source meteorological API aggregating data from official global stations. Free for non-commercial use.

summaryItems: All data sources are free and do not require API keys
EcoSense combines both sources for comprehensive global coverage
Sensor.Community provides real sensor measurements from real places
Open-Meteo ensures coverage for major cities without installed sensors
